author: @tonyponsford
text:
Hi Rob, have used a car battery to "refresh lazy magnets" in the past.
A couple of wires to the battery and momentarily "splash" the magnet. Sparks yes, shorting a battery yes, but works a treat. Not for the faint-hearted. One in particular, a huge marine diesel generator failed to start, traced it back to the permanent magnet that creates the initial flux field within the winding core..a grub screw had loosened and the magnet did not spin with the shaft and so became demagnetised. Took it off the shaft, splashed it and it ran like a good one. Same principle as yours, but loads more power :)
Regards Tony

author: @B2Hives
text:
Were there any Warnings, Cautions or Danger listed ?   A copy machine I had long ago.  there was an  Aluminum (appearing)  cylinder 5 in dia, x 17 in  length.   The warning labels stated it contained Arsenic.  Beware,  Alert and so on.  
 Your Toner Disc,   if you pass it through a magnitizer edge first,  will it polarize each half of the disc that way?    If you pass the disc through the magnitizer  flat, in the same plane as the magnitizer will each side of the disc be opposite polarity?
 Suppose you have a pyramid shape of iron,  you magnitize it,  one pole of the base would have the same volume as the pointy pole correct? Would there be any  application, use or advantage to have an odd shaped magnet such as that?   Is there a reason we have lived in the mundane world  of circle disc, cube, rectangles and horseshoe shapes?

replies.author: @rogerstarkey5390
replies.text:
Old copiers used an Aluminium cylinder (drum) coated in silicon, which was doped with arsenic.
Usually (exclusively?) Silver in colour.
The one John had is a modern "Organic Photoconductive".
Safe.
(Although I wouldn't lick it!)

author: @David_Walker16-3-51
text: Many years ago I was told to replenish the toner in the company photocopier. No idea what I was doing and of course I spilled the toner everywhere. Whoops, better clean that up, so I fetched a vacuum cleaner. The only plug socket in range was marked “Computers Only” what the hell, I’d used those before without any problem. Plugged in the vac, switched it on and sucked up the toner....... Flash! the building was plunged into darkness, quickly followed by cries of rage from the Accounts dept the Sales dept and the CEO’s office. I was not popular. Didn’t know toner was magnetic but I certainly found out it was conductive!

replies.text:
Yep.
That's how it works.
Electrostatically charged toner attracted to a slightly charged magnetic carrier, but pulled away to an "opposite" (much different) charge potential on exposed areas of the photoconductive drum....... Then, other stuff.

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering oh yeah additionally I should point out, that I have a video up somewhere of me taking a hard drive armature magnet, crushing it with a hammer, and then mixing the powder into epoxy and placing that in a ziplock baggie, that I closed up and then placed on a whole still magnetized armature magnet end (each end is magnetized in opposite directions so they are basically like two magnets side by side, facing opposite directions with left say being north and right being south, and if you look at the other side reverse that, anyway) that way it would all be facing one direction, and as a result the magnetic powder formed spikes in the epoxy, so the side with the base of the spikes sticks to steel, but the other side will not and even refuses to stick to the point I have had it roll up across an iron anvil in order to stick to the other side, (had my finger under it to keep it from rolling down ward and as soon as I moved my hand off the magnet holding it down with the spike tips toward the steel ,it sprung up ward and stuck down with the other side, so I am guessing the other pole on the spike tip side is also forming similar pole magnetic energy facing outward from the sides of the spikes or something because it did not even have an effect to pull on thin steel shim when I had it facing that side holding it above the steel, but I could get it to grab the steel up off the table when I held it the other way, from nearly 4 times the distance

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: there is toner in it too because it covers the whole plastic sheet that applies the toner to the paper and the laser effects this causing it to fall off where not wanted, which is then sent to a "WASTE TONER" compartment, and it does not get reused by the cartridge, so if you printed only text, then calculate how much of the paper is covered in toner, and then subtract that from 100% and you get how much of the toner you had starting is now in the waste bin when it shows up as being empty IF it were actually completely and utterly empty, which you pointed out is usually not the case LOL. anyway, the heat to fuse it is a wire that heats up so the laser is not used to fuse it to the paper, it just pumps the film causing it to become conductive and the reverse polarity of the sheet is in a roller that pushes through that conductive bit causing the toner to either not transfer or to fall completely off and into that waste unit, I have a TON of it in my waste compartment that removing it allowed me to run more copies and since its basically powdered plastic stuff that heat melts it, I hung onto a bunch of it XD

replies.author: @rogerstarkey5390
replies.text:
Sorry, wrong.
All of the toner is held on the magnetic ("Mag") roller in the development unit.
Toner is only attracted to areas of the photoconductive drum discharged by the laser. (Called the "latent image")
Paper is pressed against that drum surface with a "different" charge applied to the back face, attracting most, but not all of the toner.
Any residual toner on the drum is physically removed by a rubber blade and static brush.
That's the waste toner.
It's usually contaminated with paper dust, so isn't reused.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome - now some uses for it lol

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: @rogerstarkey5390 ah I knew there was waste toner in the unit when it was used thus not all was getting put on the paper and the extra was only because it stops working before it runs out. however I have toner that is not magnetic at all, and there for there is no "mag" roller in the unit, period, so how is that one working? also I new the laser had something to do with the roller that the paper touched was only coated on part of it, while a whole roller was getting coated as if you were printing a giant square or rectangle of color on the paper, and all this extra that is not going onto the paper was not just being dumped back into the toner drum to be used again later.

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering yeah I thought some time ago about using a laser to fuse it together and then roll out another layer by using a spreader that rises up an increment at a time between spreading a layer of the toner so a laser can move around and fuse part of it together, and now I have quite a bit of all 4 colors of toner that laser printers come with, (none of it has any magnetic properties, I have tested it, both the fused and unfused LOL)

author: @BaldyTron
text: Could you powder coat with toner

replies.author: @rogerstarkey5390
replies.text:
It's essentially a similar process, but I imagine the formulation of the coating is different.
When you agitate "toner" (the plastic element) it attains a "triboelectric charge" (rub a balloon on your hair, same thing)
In a copier, that charge sticks it to the magnetic material which is then held on the magnetic roller Robert found.
In powder coating I imagine the powder (similar to *only* the toner, no magnetic component) will be stirred to charge, sucked into the  spray head and sprayed at the 
 object, which had charge applied through the hanging wire(?)

Alternativly you could use a boron/niodinuim ceramic magnet in the oven under a silicone dish of toner as they can keep their magnetism upto around 280⁰c where as most niodinium magnets loose their pep at 80⁰C...

Just a thought for easier than having a home kiln magnet making.

author: @terrygee210
text:
A question.  I understand that magnetisation works by aligning domains in the target material.
 Does this weaken the donor magnet  when "Stroke" magnetising?

replies.author: @hanslepoeter5167
replies.text: In principle yes. In reality very little

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes - but it will be years before you notice it

replies.author: @Merlin3189
replies.text:
IMO no.  We used to use "keepers" - bars of soft iron put between the poles of permanent magnets when not in use.  When a magnet pole is put near a piece of iron or other magnetic material, it increases the field inside the magnet as well as in the iron, because the magnetic circuit is better.  
People often think there is some quantity of magnetism inside a magnet which can be used up.  But it is all down to alignment.  Anything which increases the magnetic flux through the magnet, will help keep or increase the alignment of the domains.   Air between the poles is very impermeable, so any part of the gap being filled with iron makes it more permeable and increases the flux.

A friend recently restored two outboard motors with "dead" magnetos.  I thought you would need to remove the magnet and remagnetise it (as it said in the manual), but he just got his electric drill, spun the magnetos for a few minutes and sparks reappeared.  The magnet was good as ever.

replies.author: @jeffreyrood8755
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I have a big microwave magnet swinging over the coil off a synchronous motor like you used. I hooked up 2 more small coils in series to get more out of this set up. It is putting out pretty decent numbers but I think a stronger coil would be ideal. I think It would be better than the in series set i have now.

replies.author: @JavierChiappa
replies.text:
Well if you can gut a microwave transformer, those have a very bulky primary winding that might be a good already made coil.  The primary coil has a very thick wire that can handle a lot of current. The secondary winding has many more turns of a thinner wire. Those are your extremes of coils. Try both of those to see which one has a better output for your application! 

In your case, try to look in youtube the term "axial flux wind turbine permanent magnet generator", and use that way to build your coils and permanent magnets, since it's way more efficient. Hope it works!

author: @rogerstarkey5390
text:
Robert.
We call the magnetic/ plastic mix "developer"
The "toner" is the plastic component
The green tube is the photoconductive "drum" which has a charge applied, then is etched by laser.
The "plastic" component (toner) is pre charged in the unit then attracted  by the potential difference of the etched (0v) areas of the drum. The magnetic carrier remains in the Development unit.

replies.text: You left out the corona wire part.

replies.author: @city_viking
replies.text: There are two types of toner.  Mono component has magnetic toner while dual has a separate magnetic component called developer and just toner  In the later the toner is just plastic and carbon.  Sharp uses separate components and Canon uses mono component.  A lot of the smaller printers use mono component. In dual component printers you can actually feel the grains of developer.  It feels like sand mixed with toner.

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Awesome! I've been thinking about making  homemade ferrite magnetic cores with magnetite and sodium silicate,  But the toner plastic is an immensly interesting idea. I hope it's nonconductive.   

Time to fuse toner ink in a bun cake mold for a toroid transformer and play with it.

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: Your average toner does not conduct, but it does hold a static charge (required for the printing process).  However, I don't know about toner that contains magnetite.  I would certainly test it before trying to use it for non-conductive applications. 😸

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: @NorthernKitty plastic coating nonconductive Makes sense  for static photo sensitive printing.

replies.author: @rogerstarkey5390
replies.text:
@NorthernKitty 
I suspect there will be sufficient toner to cover the developer, making in non-conductive, no guarantee that will be 100%.

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: ​@kreynolds1123  It's been decades since I repaired laser printers, so I wasn't sure if anything had changed with how they attract the toner to the drum.  If I recall, it used to be that the toner was separate from the magnetite. I believe the magnetite coated the developer drum and helped the toner to adhere once it was drawn to the drum.  I could be wrong, it's been a very long time.  I don't know how it works when the two are mixed together from the start.  But I know they are now experimenting in adding metal oxides like magnetite to polymers to make flexible transistors for wearable electronics.  So it makes me suspect that there's some degree to which magnetite in toner could be conductive.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: @NorthernKitty magnetite on it's own is slightly conductive. But if it is coated with plastic,  it wouldn't be conductive unless the concentration of magnetite were such that each magnetite particle were touching enough other magnetite particles to make a low resistance circuit.  All it would take is just a reasonable level of resistance to minimize eddy currents.
